lede: Buddhist Canonical · พระไตรปิฎก › Vinaya accounts for 494 catalogued manuscripts. It clusters in Phrae (42% of the corpus for this sub-genre), ahead of Lampang and Chiang Mai. Nearly all (82%) are written in Tham Lanna · อักษรธรรมล้านนา script. By support it leans to palm-leaf · ใบลาน (93%) over mulberry paper (saa) · กระดาษสา (6%). Dated witnesses run 1537–1973 CE (378 of 494 carry a date).

findings.title: The deepest roots in the collection
findings.gloss: The ten earliest-dated manuscripts, reaching back to 1471 CE — the far anchor of the single continuous timeline that runs all the way to today's amulet stalls.

authored.title: Buddhist Canonical › Vinaya
authored.body_html:
What this is

This is the discipline basket — the Vinayapiṭaka, the monastic code: the rules by which the saṅgha governs itself, and the second-largest canonical sub-genre at 494 witnesses. But the striking thing about the Lanna vinaya is that a large part of it is not rules to be read but liturgy to be performed — the working ritual of monastic life.

Two faces: the code and the rite

On the scholastic side sit the classical texts: the Pācittiya (the offences requiring expiation), the Pārivāra (the appendix-digest), the Mahāvagga, and above all the great commentary, the Samantapāsādika (Samantapāsādika, Samanta) — the vinaya as a body of law and its exegesis.

On the performed side sit the ordination texts, and these are the living end of the basket: the kammavācā (Kammawa, Nangsue kammavācā) — the formal acts of the saṅgha — and the upasampadā ordination liturgy (Witthi uppasombot, "the procedure for higher ordination"). These are vinaya as spoken rite: the exact words by which a novice becomes a monk. That is why this sub-genre reaches across into the chanting liturgy — the kammavācā is a recitation before it is a rule.

The shape of the collection

Physically canonical — 93% palm-leaf (461 of 494) — and well-dated, 378 witnesses spanning 1537–1973. Like the sutta it concentrates at Wat Sung Men (199, ~40%) with Phrae and Lampang behind, the keystone-library effect again. Its language tilt is notably Pali-heavy: 199 monolingual Pali against 272 Pali-and-Lan Na — the formal legal and liturgical Pali needs no gloss to be recited correctly.

Notes

  • The ordination liturgy is the part that matters most to a living tradition. The kammavācā and uppasombot texts are the operative script of an act still performed today; read them beside the liturgy corpus, where the same recitational logic governs the paritta and thon.
  • Separate the code from the rite. The Pācittiya/Samantapāsādika legal core and the kammavācā/uppasombot performance texts are two different objects under one "Vinaya" label; pulling them apart is the obvious next pass and would move a good share of this sub-genre toward liturgy.
  • Not research-priority, but structurally central. None of the 494 are flagged; its claim is that it holds the constitutional and ceremonial machinery of the saṅgha — the rules that made the copyists themselves.
